data.title: Oakmark Equity And Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth Quarter, 2025

  • The Fund (Investor Class) outperformed the benchmark, the 60% S&P 500 / 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter and since inception.
  • The equity portfolio returned 5.081 for the quarter versus 2.66% for the S&P 500 Index. The fixed income portfolio returned 1.35% versus 1.10% for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The Fund’s allocation was 60.0% in equities, 38.5% in fixed income, and 1.5% cash.
  • At the sector level for the equity portfolio, the largest contributors to performance were communication services and health care, while industrials was the only detractor.
  • The Fund had a strong quarter as the breadth of the market rally improved, with many value and smaller capitalization stocks performing well. We continue to find attractive opportunities to invest in undervalued companies across a variety of industries, including areas that have been left behind in the past year’s momentum rally.

Fourth-quarter highlights

Top stock contributors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG)
  • Warner Bros Discovery (WBD)
  • Glencore (GLCNF)

Top stock detractors

  • Fiserv (FISV)
  • Equifax (EFX)
  • Charter Communications Cl A (CHTR)

New stock purchases

  • Amrize (AMRZ)
  • Gartner (IT)
  • Mondelēz Cl A (MDLZ)

Final stock sales

  • BlackRock (BLK)
  • Charter Communications Cl A

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Alphabet was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price jumped after it delivered impressive third-quarter earnings. Search revenue growth beat consensus expectations as Google continues to see strong user engagement trends. Results were also ahead of expectations in the Cloud segment, and the outlook for this business remains robust given accelerating demand for AI compute. We continue to believe Alphabet is undervalued on a sum-of-the-parts basis and see potential for the company’s AI leadership to drive further upside across the portfolio.

Top detractor

Fiserv was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered bank software and payment processor’s stock price declined sharply after reporting disappointing third-quarter earnings. Following the completion of a recent strategic review, Fiserv’s new CEO concluded that prior leadership’s medium-term revenue growth and margin targets were unachievable. While we are disappointed by these revelations, we continue to believe that Fiserv is a formidable competitor with attractive assets operating in growing end markets. We are also encouraged by recent upgrades to the executive management team and the board of directors, as well as recent insider stock purchases. The new management team expects Fiserv to deliver mid-single-digit organic revenue growth and double-digit earnings per share (EPS) growth on a normalized basis. Accordingly, we believe the risk/reward is attractive with the shares trading at a single-digit multiple of current EPS.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

Amrize is a leading producer of building materials and construction solutions, established following Holcim's spin-off of its North American operations in June. As a standalone company, Amrize holds strong market positions—it is the largest cement producer, the second-largest commercial roofing products manufacturer and a top-five aggregates producer in the U.S. and Canada. The company benefits from pricing power due to its scale, advantaged regional footprint and tight supply dynamics. We also see long-term potential in the Building Envelope segment and believe there is a path to narrowing the margin gap to roofing peers. In our view, Amrize is a fundamentally strong business that is flying under the radar of most U.S. investors. This created the opportunity to invest alongside a proven management team focused on unlocking shareholder value.

Gartner is a global leader in research services, with a long history of delivering valuable insights and data to business and technology leaders. In our view, the company has the best brand in IT research, supported by its scale and a compelling customer value proposition. These advantages have driven a long history of strong organic growth and robust free-cash-flow conversion. The stock price has declined meaningfully from recent highs due to investor concerns surrounding AI-related disruption. We believe these concerns are overstated. In our view, Gartner is well-positioned to reaccelerate organic growth due to continued high customer engagement and the large opportunity to sell to new and existing customers. We took advantage of the opportunity to buy shares in this well-managed company at a bargain price.

Mondelēz International is a global snacking powerhouse with leading market share positions in crackers, cookies and chocolate. The brand portfolio houses iconic names like Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, Oreo and Ritz. Mondelēz possesses a unique global footprint that over-indexes to snacking occasions. Snacking is an advantaged category that benefits from robust pricing power, low private label competition and rising per capita consumption. We believe these attributes will help Mondelēz sustain industry-leading growth. A rapid rise in commodity costs has temporarily depressed margins, masking the company’s true earnings power. We believe Mondelēz’s strong pricing power and commodity relief will help improve margins. The short-term fears surrounding commodity inflation allowed us to purchase shares at a discounted valuation relative to history, peers and the broader market.

data.title: Inflation Is Sticky, Yet The Market Doesn't Care - Or Does It?
data.description:
December CPI data is out, and the results are slightly better than what the market expected:

  • Core CPI YoY 2.6% vs. 2.7% expected

  • Core CPI MoM 0.2% vs. 0.3% expected

With inflation still well above the Fed’s theoretical 2% target, it would be fair to think we are seeing a sticky metric. That is even more evident when putting the latest data in a 1-year context:

For the entirety of 2025, US inflation has been well above 2% and much closer to 3% (which, I argued in the past, may anyway turn out to be the Fed’s new target). The last time CPI was below the 2% target was February 2021, almost five years ago. In other words, we have now gone almost half a decade with inflation above target.

Despite this, equity markets have been rallying since late 2023 (even if through a few deep but quick corrections, like that of April 2025). Have markets been wrong in predicting lower inflation (and lower interest rates) for more than two years? Or have they simply stopped caring about inflation?

Today, I argue markets do care about inflation. And they have been right about seeing it cool off. However, they have been too early in discounting a return to a long-term Zero-Interest-Rate policy.

US domestic disinflationary forces will manifest in 2026

There are a number of reasons to be positive about inflation coming down in the next few months.

First of all, there is the impact of US trade tariffs on inflation. With most tariffs in full effect since August, I deem it unlikely we will see any spike in consumer prices in the upcoming months due to tariffs. While personally I remain of the opinion tariffs serve no purpose and provide no upside to Americans, I recognize their inflationary effect has most likely run its course.

And that’s without considering the option that tariffs may even get rolled back as a result of the Supreme Court declaring them “illegal” in their current form. If that were to happen, it would represent not a neutral but a disinflationary matter.

Then, we have the situation with oil, Venezuela, and Russia. Oil prices have a direct connection with consumer inflation, and they are close to their lowest in five years. Lower prices are at least partially a result of the current administration’s recent actions, as well as declining global demand (on the back of secular disinflationary forces I will discuss shortly).

Venezuela’s US-led regime change, while still ongoing, may help unlock oil supply that was previously severely limited due to US sanctions. Additionally, America seizing the “shadow fleet” of Russia may put pressure on Russia’s regime to bring the war in Ukraine to an end. Were that to happen, and were sanctions on Russia to be scaled back as a result, we would see even more natural gas and oil reaching the market.

Importantly, there is also the issue of the labor market. The Fed’s mandate is not exclusively about inflation but equally about full employment. With unemployment data creeping up closer to 5%, the Fed may have further room to cut rates, regardless of whether inflation stays closer to 3% than their target. In fact, a weak job market has been one of the reasons why equity markets kept rallying in late 2025, despite somewhat sticky inflation.

Purely in terms of inflation, a weak labor market may also act as a disinflationary force per se, as people out of a job (or earning less in real terms) tend to consume less. While US wage growth has so far kept up with inflation, this may also change with the advent of the next AI wave. AI has already been blamed for replacing jobs, even if I think evidence at the macro level remains thin so far. 2026 will be the year when we finally understand the full impact of AI on employment and productivity.

Finally, there are some of Trump’s recent policies, including the idea of blocking institutional investors from buying homes. In my view, it is clear some of the administration’s recent moves are geared towards fighting inflation, potentially ahead of Mid-Terms. It remains to be seen to what extent these initiatives may help, but I think we are now seeing an administration that is actively trying to fight inflation.

Global demographic forces remain a secular tailwind for America

I find it is sometimes easy to get lost in analyzing US-specific data without considering the broader global picture. The US only weighs about 25% of the world’s GDP. Even in the (arguably) post-globalization world we live in, America is still very much influenced by global trends.

I see the US as a unicum, in that it is a developed, large, high-growth, highly productive country with demographic issues that are not nearly as bad as those in most other developed countries. The rest of the world looks very, very different.

Demographic declines are a reality everywhere except in Africa. The US is the only developed, large country that is set to remain where it is in terms of number of people in 2100 (see infochart below).

A collapsing demographic is a strong disinflationary (and potentially even deflationary) force.

Readers can look at the examples of Japan and China, with the first having already undergone a major demographic collapse and China having recently been overtaken by India (assuming official Chinese numbers are even real in the first place). Japan has seen very low inflation for the past three decades, while China is currently battling deflation. In my view, Europe is set to follow suit in the next decade, representing yet another global disinflationary force. Even India, the world’s most populous country today, is set to see its population shrinking in the upcoming decades.

America will benefit from these trends in terms of inflation. Indirectly, via a decrease in demand for commodities and energy. As well as directly, with other nations exporting to its vibrant domestic market for lack of a sufficiently large domestic market.

Risks: The AI question and a stagflationary scenario

The main wild card for 2026, in my view, remains AI. AI may help America get out of sticky inflation, or it may worsen the situation. The below table, taken from my previous work, summarizes the possible effects of AI scenarios on the job market, inflation, and equities.

A stagflationary scenario would be the worst possible outcome for the US economy in 2026. This may manifest if, for example, AI productivity gains “overheat” the economy and cause inflation while not leading to mass unemployment. AI demand in itself, barring any other effect, may turn inflationary by virtue of increased energy demand (a core component of inflation).

With such uncertainty, I invite readers to review my 2026 market outlook article. There, I discuss in depth the economic and market implications of AI developing in 2026 and reaching a level close to AGI (Artificial General Intelligence).

Conclusion: markets are right, inflation is cooling off.

Barring an AI wild card, I think we are going to see inflation subdued in 2026, courtesy of:

  • Tariffs-induced inflation has run its course

  • Cheaper oil prices

  • A weaker labor market

  • Secular global disinflationary forces on the back of a global demographic crisis

Truflation, a proprietary inflation metric that is supposed to be less lagging than CPI, is already reporting data below 2%.

While this indicator has been way more volatile than CPI, it has done a decent job in recent years at “anticipating” future readings. For example, Truflation was already below 2% for a few weeks in late Q1 2025, before Trump’s “Liberation Day”.

In this context, I remain bullish on the S&P 500 and, more broadly, on US equities. I think markets have been correctly discounting a return to ZIRP, but they have so far been wrong about timing. 2026 will prove them right.

data.title: ClearBridge Global Infrastructure Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Listed infrastructure trailed global equities in the fourth quarter, both delivering a more subdued end to a strong year of performance.
  • Contributors: On a regional basis, Western Europe was the top portfolio contributor for quarter, with U.K. electric utility SSE and Spanish electric utility Iberdrola the lead performers.
  • Detractors: Higher production and storage levels and weaker weather forecasts for natural gas kept prices lower most of the quarter, weighing on shares of natural gas utilities and pipeline stocks. Elevated interest rates and lower growth drove largely negative performance from communication tower stocks.
  • Outlook: Inflection in electricity demand and solid earnings growth helped listed infrastructure performance in 2025, and this looks set to continue in 2026, with the added benefit of lower nominal bond yields.

Performance Review

  • On a regional basis, Western Europe was the top portfolio contributor for quarter, with U.K. electric utility SSE and Spanish electric utility Iberdrola the lead performers.
  • SSE (SSEZY) is a diversified energy utility headquartered in Scotland. It is vertically integrated, operating over the entire supply chain in the U.K., with generation (including hydro, wind, combined cycle gas turbine), electricity networks, and retail businesses (primarily B2B). It is the U.K.’s largest renewable energy generator. SSE’s share price rose as funding risks diminished and concerns around the U.K. macroeconomic outlook eased.
  • Iberdrola (IBDSF) is a multinational integrated electric utility company headquartered in Spain and engaged in energy networks, renewables and wholesale and retail operations. The company has expanded internationally with operations in the U.K. (via Scottish Power), the U.S. (via Avangrid), Brazil (via Neoenergia (NRGIY)) and Mexico. Iberdrola delivered a solid capital markets day, with management raising guidance again at its third-quarter results.
  • U.S. electric utility OGE Energy (OGE) and Spanish electric utility Redeia (RDEIF) were the largest detractors.
  • OGE Energy operates the largest utility business in Oklahoma. Delays in the announcement of a data center deal weighed on shares during the quarter.
  • Redeia is engaged in the supply and transmission of electricity and is the sole high-voltage transmission agent and system operator in Spain. Negative sentiment surrounding a suboptimal outcome from the Spanish regulatory review for 2026–2031 weighed on Redeia’s share price.

Outlook

  • Inflection in electricity demand and solid earnings growth helped listed infrastructure performance in 2025, and this looks set to continue in 2026, with the added benefit of lower nominal bond yields.
  • Electric utilities, which make up the bulk of our portfolio, continue to bene1t from several tailwinds: the energy transition, as poles and wires are built out to connect renewables to the grid and EV charging stations (all regulated expenditure for utilities and earning regulated returns); the mitigation of and adaptation to climate change impacts on electricity networks, which is driving resiliency spend; and growing electricity demand, particularly from AI data centers, but also from growing industrial demand. These tailwinds support a strong earnings backdrop and continued conviction in a balanced exposure to utilities.

data.title: Plains All Americans: The High-Yield Crude Option
data.description:
Introduction

Plains All American (NASDAQ: PAA/PGP) has gone through a consequent strategic move, narrowing its pure-play crude focus since I last reviewed the company back in early August. Though it has appreciated on par with the S&P00 since, I want to see if the company has made the required improvements to become a buy in my books. Right now, Plains is one of the largest midstream companies in the country that has a great presence in the Permian and an 18,000+ mile pipeline network.

Current Dynamics

Following its Canadian NGL divestment earlier in 2025, PAA has gone further into being a pure-play crude midstream as it markets itself. PAA bought the EPIC crude pipeline to further dominate the Permian. PAA executed a two-step takeover for a total of $2.9B, including debt, and will integrate it into its network as Cactus III. The advantage, aside from increased volume throughput, would be that the company can now switch barrels between lines to optimize flow, reducing the use of expensive drag-reducing agents and offering more flexible shipping options to different export docks in Corpus Christi.

Current capacity for the line is set at roughly 600K bpd but could be expanded to 900K simply by adding more pumping stations, so PAA could incur low expansion costs if there is still demand for it. Although the company will pay the sellers up to $350MM in addition to earnout payments if it does so. The deal is set to trade at around 10x EBITDA multiples that can drop to 8x thanks to synergies and the expected increase in throughput.

There is some good and some bad with the deal in my opinion, as the company now controls the most efficient route from the Permian to the export hubs of Corpus Christi, leaving the ability to bundle their services for big producers. It will be immediately accretive to DCF and PAA is expecting mid-teens unlevered return on the investment. On the flip side, the deal spiked leverage for the company as it closed before the Canadian NGL sale finished, meaning that for the time being, the company’s leverage ratio is sitting above its target, though that is only transitory.

One move that the company has made to remediate this would be the one-time special distribution of around 35 cents per unit to reassure unitholders, as the Canadian NGL business sale is a taxable event. More so, the company is expecting to pay roughly $360MM in entity-level taxes in Canada, which is actually good news for US unitholders, as it will most likely generate foreign tax credits that can be passed through to unitholders and their K-1s to offset the US tax hit.

Now, as the company is positioning itself as a crude pure-play, it has become more sensitive to volume shifts in the Permian and global export demand than ever before. The Permian is still producing over 6MM bpd, but the era of double-digit annual growth is most likely over, as analysts are expecting US production to hover around 13.5MM through 2026, and that’s why the company has pivoted towards a market share approach rather than market growth.

Following the US operation in Venezuela and the current administration signalling plans to move directly 30 to 50 million barrels of heavy crude in storage, it redirects future production towards US Gulf Coast refineries. Outside of the difficulties to ramp up production in the country, if refineries can get cheap oil that is more tailored towards their complexity levels than demand for light Permian barrels might slow down, creating bottlenecks through PAA’s system.

Valuation

Plains compares fairly well with its peers with its EV/EBITDA of 9.8, which is the second lowest in the group, but where the company really shines through is its current FCF generation that allows the company to have one of the best FCF yields in the industry at 17.7%, and this trend has been going on since 2020. Right now, the company has shifted from the past growth-at-all-costs mode to a harvest mode that allows it to directly find opportunities that make FCF skyrocket. Though the company might suffer from a pure-play valuation discount that is tied to the current state of the oil market. Looking at distribution, PAA boasts one of the highest yields at 8.09%, though it slightly decreases from the 9% we’ve seen earlier in 2025. The distribution has extensively grown over the past 3 years at a rate of 22.22% CAGR, and management has announced a massive 10% distribution hike for the year that excludes the special 35 cents, which will make it a gigantic year for income-focused unitholders.

The rebranded Cactus III is the main growth engine heading into 2026, which could push earnings growth at roughly 15% per year through 2027, which is currently forecasted, though revenue growth is expected to be much flatter. Now I believe that this can change when PAA closes the $3.75B Canadian sale in the first quarter, which will significantly drop the company’s leverage ratio. The company can either use the proceeds to further expand its footprint in the Permian, boosting margins, or use that lower leverage to boost the company’s credit rating upgrades, lowering their borrowing costs, and in the end have better economics for deals further down the line.

PAA is undergoing a quality over quantity shift, and that’s why, in part, margins are fairly low, as highlighted by the 4.9% EBITDA margin. The key will be the Canadian NGL business sale, which used to be way more "commodity exposed" and pushed profits to swing widely. This is now replaced by the EPIC crude pipeline "take-or-pay" cash flow. It is also very important for investors to grasp why margins are so low compared to peers, something I did not grasp in my earlier analysis. See PAA, compared to others, acts as a massive middleman in the crude market, buying physical oil at the wellhead and selling it to refineries. If it buys $100 worth of oil and sells it for $102, it will only generate a 2% margin. While other companies in the space generally don’t buy the oil and just charge a fee to move it, thus drastically improving their margins. That’s why there is such a difference in the actual FCF PAA is generating.

Risks

The risk profile for PAA has significantly narrowed to the crude industry, mainly in the Permian. The shift towards a potentially heavier crude mix for refineries might be the biggest risk for PAA, especially if it has to lower shipping rates to keep its pipelines full. More so if Permian producers stop drilling due to the inherently lower price, then PAA could see far lower volumes in the future, thus lower sales.

It also has no Plan B if the world moves away from crude faster than expected, as the company is simply now a proxy of future volume, mainly in the Permian. I believe that the company’s position is solid, but investors fearing this should inherently choose other well-diversified midstreams.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, PAA can be a strong choice for investors looking to have a massive yield based on huge FCF generation, only tied to one industry. Mainly, if you’re bullish on the continuous volumes produced in the Permian, PAA should be your play, and it will reward you handsomely starting with its distribution. I upgrade my rating to a Buy.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion: Betting A Farm On Ethereum Is Risky
data.description:
Reasons to invest in BMNR

Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) is a crypto-mining company, which is shifting towards becoming a crypto-treasury and Ethereum (ETH-USD) stacking player. At the moment, BMNR’s biggest bet is its Ethereum-focused treasury strategy meaning accumulating vast volumes of this crypto-asset. As of today, the company has accumulated over 4.16 million tokens, which means it currently holds 3.45% of the overall token supply. The company’s ultimate goal in this direction is to hold 5% of all the tokens, which they call the ‘Alchemy of 5%’.

At the moment, value of the company’s cash and crypto-holdings totals massive $14 billion, which is higher than BMNR’s market cap of $13.3 billion. BMNR’s total liabilities as of November 30, 2025 were just $235 million, meaning that there are almost no liabilities against the company’s massive cash+crypto position. With that being said, BMNR currently trades cheaper than its book value, which looks quite attractive. I will discuss other valuation metrics in “Reasons to avoid BMNR”; however, from the P/B ratio perspective alone, it might look like a bargain.

BMNR’s heavy bet on crypto-assets has potential because of secular shifts in the global financial system. Cryptocurrencies were ignored by large institutions for quite a long time, but even they started recognizing potential of crypto assets as there are currently numerous ETF issue by quite large asset managers like BlackRock or Fidelity that invest in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or Ethereum. Some of the largest banks like JPMorgan, UBS, and Citi offer their customers crypto-related services like custody, tokenized deposits, and settlement solutions. From this viewpoint, there are solid broad crypto-related tailwinds behind BMNR.

And BMNR is not just accumulating all these vast ETH-USD amounts but is preparing infrastructure to make this assets work, which is called ‘stacking’ in the crypto-world. By stacking, it means that BMNR’s large crypto deposits will be supporting the entire ecosystem of Ethereum, which is an activity that generates income. For this purpose, the company is building the Made in America Validator Network (“MAVAN”), which is expected to be launched with commercial operations during Q1 2026. Having this system will allow the company to generate staking rewards rather than just holding these assets passively. Having a recurring revenue source is always good from the fundamental perspective as it gives greater visibility for the management and investors.

Finally, there are a few quite successful examples when crypto-miners shifted to the AI infrastructure business, and some of them already boast contracts with tech giants. For example, IREN Limited (IREN) is a also a crypto-mining company with own powerful compute capacity. As a result, they currently have a multi-billion AI infrastructure contract with Microsoft (MSFT). There are other examples of crypto-mining companies inking AI infrastructure deal with smaller players compared to Microsoft, but still, they are multi-billion commitments. These examples are companies like Core Scientific (CORZ) or Hut 8 (HUT). The management of BMNR didn't share plans to reposition part of its assets for AI infrastructure business, and I am not sure that they will be able to compete considering the fact that the AI infrastructure space is becoming crowded. However, the topic is extremely hot as investors are extremely excited about AI infrastructure companies. Therefore, just an announcement that the company is considering strategic shift to expand AI infrastructure foothold might trigger a strong upward share price movement.

Reasons to avoid BMNR

Now let's talk about reasons to avoid BMNR, as there are a few large ones. While secular trends are positive for the crypto-industry overall, I find Ethereum-focused growth strategy too risky. Above all, keeping more than 90% of the company's assets just in one instrument means enormous concentration risks. Moreover, Ethereum is not the world's largest crypto-asset. Its current market cap is less than Bitcoin's by almost five times, and betting a farm on the laggard looks like a really risky strategy. Bitcoin's larger market cap means that it is stronger as a brand and it has more recognition among investors. Cryptocurrencies do not have intrinsic value, meaning that brand and recognition are extremely important for attracting new investors.

Moreover, the company's management plans to continue expanding the Ethereum exposure. As I mentioned earlier, their ultimate goal is to achieve "The Alchemy of 5%", which means they plan to invest substantial amounts to expand the ETH-USD position. As I said earlier, the company's liabilities are extremely low, while its cash from operations is slightly below zero. It means that aggressive shareholder dilution is the only source to finance the aggressive ETH-USD purchasing strategy. Since the company plans to significantly expand its crypto-holdings further, we will certainly see more dilution. The management pushes shareholders to vote for approval of the charter amendment to increase the number of authorized shares from 500 million to 50 billion. The authorization itself does not 100% mean that the management will issue billions of new shares at once, but it will give them green light to issue new shares in almost infinite volumes.

Moreover, Ethereum's price is in a notable downtrend despite a 10% YTD rebound. It traded closer to $5,000 in late summer 2025, while the current price is approximately $3,300. The company's latest reportable quarter ended on November 30, when ETH-USD price was even lower, below $3,000. As a result, BMNR recorded a massive $5+ billion P&L loss on revaluation of its digital assets during the quarter. Of course, it is a non-cash item, which makes the loss not that bad from fundamental perspective. However, having such large swings in the balance value of its core asset means that we should better look at other valuation ratios as well, not just the P/B ratio.

The company's TTM revenue is almost zero as the MAVAN product is still not ready. It is expected to go live in Q1, meaning that the company will see a notable revenue increase due to stacking in FY2026. However, even if stacking revenue targets are achieved in FY2026 and FY2027, the forward P/S ratio of somewhere around 30 means that valuation is extremely aggressive. Moreover, BMNR's stacking revenue is expected to be measured in millions while we see that capital losses from ETH-USD can be in billions in just one quarter. It means that if Ethereum's share price weakness continues, the company will generate substantial bottom-line losses.

Moreover, there are other risks to ETH-USD price. For example, the 14-day RSI indicator looks overheated after the rebound from mid-December lows. In addition, ETH-USD failed twice to break through the $3,300 resistance level since early December, and it is approaching this psychological level again. If it fails to break the resistance level for the third time within just five weeks, it might make investors really disappointed and could lead to a sell-off.

Besides these risks from the technical analysis perspective, there is a big structural issue. I do not want to pretend like I am a cryptography expert, but I have read various sources saying that there are quantum computing vulnerabilities for Ethereum. Any ETH-USD wallet has a private key, from which a cryptography creates a public key. Regular computers are unable to reverse-engineer the private key from the public one. However, experts say that quantum computers will be able to do it with Shor's algorithm. According to Deloitte, 65% of ETH-USD is exposed to this threat. Of course, Ethereum's developers are probably already working on solutions to mitigate this substantial risk, and we are still at least a few years away from widespread adoption of quantum computers. However, since this risk has a massive potential magnitude, it can make investors really cautious, resulting in a weaker ETH-USD price.

The verdict

I think that BMNR is valued like we are not amid the environment of weak crypto-prices with future risks, particularly for Ethereum. The potential massive dilution makes the company's current $13 billion market cap even less justifiable. On the other hand, prices of crypto-assets are inherently unpredictable and there might be swift share price movements due to some unexpected positive headlines. Therefore, I prefer to sit on the fence and give BMNR a Hold rating.

data.title: Games Workshop: Great H1 For The UK IP (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This is the third time I have written about Games Workshop's (OTCPK:GMWKF). So far, my rating record is being quite satisfactory, but the merit is not mine, it is the management and the great business execution that the owners of Warhammer are carrying out.

Today's article will focus on the company's H1 results. GAW does not hold any kind of Conference Call, but the file they publish with their results contains more than enough information. I will show it in the article, but there are some comments that really show the brilliant corporate culture that this company has.

If you want a good understanding of the company's business model and many examples of the company's culture, as well as the company's KPIs, I highly recommend you read the other two articles, I am very proud of them.

H1 Earnings

Here is an image with the company's Highlights, but we will dig deeper into some of them.

The company posted profitable sales growth in its 23 core countries and across its three channels. Sales growth was driven by the weekly launch of new miniatures across the entire scope of its intellectual property. The company also continues to geographically expand its network of company-owned stores, albeit at a slightly slower pace than initially planned, with confidence of catching up.

During H1, core revenue growth (+17.3 %) was sustained in the wholesale (+25.2 %), company-owned stores (+5.4 %) and online (+4.0 %) channels.

Core gross margin increased from 67.5 % to 69.4 %. The additional cost of the new tariffs was mitigated by manufacturing and warehousing cost efficiencies achieved through higher sales volumes, a reduction in inventory provisions (compared to the same period last year) and favorable freight rates and raw material costs. Staff costs have increased reflecting pay reviews andthe investment in new roles.

I think this is the first company I have seen that provides so much information about the items that make up the COGS, something that speaks very well of the company's transparency. We will see more examples in the rest of the article.

Core operating profit is up £28.0 million to £126.1 million and core operating profit to sales ratio is up 3.5% to 39.9%. Licensing revenue has decreased by £14.1 million to £16.0 million, due to a reduction in earned income during the period, the prior period included the royalty income earned on the launch of Space Marine 2. 86% is from PC and console game licenses.

It should be remembered that this licensing segment has operating margins of 90%, but certainly, there is not much forward visibility on them. Amazon's Warhammer series is still being developed, but for the time being it will be a non-recurring revenue that will depend somewhat on annual releases.

If we talk about sales channels, outlets globally increased by c.500 accounts to c.8,600. In the period they opened, including nine relocations, 17 stores. After closing 12 stores the total number of stores at the end of the period was 575.

Other Highlights

Games Workshop loves Warhammer and everything they do with it. Their mission is to create the best miniatures and roleplaying in the world and they are completely obsessed with preserving the hobby.

In a world where they are fighting for constant attention, Warhammer is a rare bird. A slow, expensive, difficult game with a steep learning curve and yet it still manages to gather millions of fans all over the world, a number that is only growing.

The management is aware of this, that is why they carry out several initiatives, in order to preserve the terminal value of the Hobby.

They continue to offer free introductory experiences: receive your first model, learn how to build and paint it, and play an exciting game with store staff

In fact, I was able to see this for myself in one of the stores in Madrid. They gave me two figures and told me to come back another day to paint them. I never did it, but I'll keep a phrase that the clerk told me: "We are not merchants, we are recruiters." Sublime, corporate culture in all layers of the organization.

Moreover, in order to preserve the hobby for generations and above all, to build loyalty among young people who grow up with the hobby and thus become the longest-lived customers in the world, The Warhammer Alliance schools programme has c.6,600 active school and library clubs signed up worldwide, supporting young people in improving their engineering, arts, and maths skills.

All of these is pretty amazing to me and something I love to learn from.

Capital Allocation

The company approved a new capital investment project for the opening of a Warhammer World format location in North America, scheduled to open in 2027. Moreover, and also in the same location, North America retail team are stil looking forward to finding a new location for a café format store on the east coast.

Alongside this, they have redesigned the layout of their existing Factory 2 to allow more injection moulding machines to be installed. They now have 62 injection moulding machines, up seven in the period reported.

This level of detail, together with the fact that they emphasize that they have created 90 new parking spaces next to their offices, reflects, once again, the degree of commitment to employee welfare and good corporate culture that the company has.

Changes in working capital in each period contributed £23.1 million. These changes are mainly related to license fees, which decreased by £25.5 million as a result of the high level of royalty income earned in the prior period. Warhammer retail stores have an advantage in the form of an online store, acting as a virtual stockroom portal, allowing them to offer the widest possible Warhammer range to every customer. This helps them with inventory management, especially the smaller owners, not just the company as a whole.

The company maintains a liquidity cushion of £85.0 million.

Valuation

After the H1 results, it is clear that Games Workshop's figures have changed for the better, but even so, to make this assessment I will use the 2025 annual figures they presented 6 months ago, in order to be conservative.

At the current prices at which Games Workshop is trading (£183) the market is discounting 14% growth in its FCF. I have not projected buybacks, dividends will grow at almost the same rate as FCF, the exit multiple I have used is 22X and the minimum internal rate of return I require from the investment is 12%.

I believe Games Workshop is currently at Fair Value, as I believe this is the growth rate at which this figure could increase. Sales I believe could grow at 10%, and cash will depend a lot on licensing revenues, which are uncertain at the moment. For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a hold.

Risks

The company incurred approximately £6.0 million during the reporting period as a direct consequence of the US tariff changes. The impact on gross margin has been more than offset by efficiency improvements, price increases of approximately 3.5% in miniatures and books, greater stability in raw material prices and lower inventory write-down adjustments.

AI, a very broad topic and, frankly, the company does not consider itself an expert on the subject. While it has some specialized senior managers, none yet show significant enthusiasm about it. An internal policy has been agreed to guide the entire organization, which is currently very cautious; for example, no AI-generated content or use of AI in design processes is allowed, nor is unauthorized use outside of Games Workshop, including any competition-related activity.

And to remember the rest of the risks of my previous articles, which are still valid, but far from materializing.

Warhammer competes with many alternatives for consumers’ limited free time. Preparing an army to play requires a significant investment of both time and money. With the rise of countless entertainment options featuring sci-fi content—like YouTube, Twitch, or Netflix—this could limit how much time players dedicate to the hobby. That said, there aren’t many physical fiction-based entertainment alternatives. Perhaps card games like Magic: The Gathering, although I believe the player base for these games doesn't significantly overlap. Management should continue its strategy of making the games more accessible, easier to play, and more engaging—aligned with the trend they've followed since 2016.

Poor execution of a new edition of Warhammer—especially Warhammer 40k—could also pose a risk. Since the arrival of the new management team and their closer relationship with fans, recent editions have been very successful. However, a poorly executed edition could result in weak performance for one or two fiscal years and potentially cause a sharp negative impact on the share price.

The upcoming Amazon series, for example, might not resonate with fans and could damage the IP and the company's image. This is something Warhammer has always been very protective of and will need to handle with great care. While the “Licensing” segment represents a major opportunity for the company, I also believe it carries considerable risk if not managed properly.

Conclusions

GAW is a company that I really enjoy following and I am very surprised by its great corporate culture. Their product I believe is unique in the world and has positive optionalities in the future, in the form of licensing revenue. I think it is currently well valued, so I will wait for better opportunities to add it to my portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: One Stop Systems: Focus On Profitability And Valuation, Not Growth
data.description:
One Stop Systems, Inc. (OSS) is a small company. The company’s niche is to design and manufacture computer hardware for edge computing applications – in practice, OSS produces compute and storage server frames for harsh environments, including for the defense sector, industrial use, and for autonomous cars among other use purposes. OSS also previously owned Bressner that focuses on industrial and commercial applications in the European market, but divested the business at the end of 2025.

Increasing use of edge computing for AI and machine learning, and increasing military spending, have boded well for OSS. The company’s bookings have grown significantly, leading to a good revenue growth outlook and expanding margins. The stock’s performance clearly reflects the fact – OSS has surged by 265% during the past year. Yet, given OSS’s poor earnings track record and a weak competitive advantage, I believe that OSS is too risky to invest in especially at the current valuation.

AI Edge Computing, Military Spending Boost OSS’s Growth Outlook

There’s clear reason for optimism around OSS. The overall AI edge computing market is projected to grow at a 22.5% CAGR from 2025 to 2032, and military edge computing is expected to show a good 15.9% CAGR during the next decade. Militaries are utilizing a growing amount of digital infrastructure in operations, and autonomous driving requires additional computing power in vehicles. OSS’s core offering is positioned well to capture new deals in the growing markets.

Increasing overall military spending also aids OSS as defense makes up a significant portion of OSS’s customer base. The U.S. defense budget was increased by 8% to $901 billion for 2026, growing at a rate clearly above inflation. Trump’s ambition for military spending is even higher; Trump has recently called for a massive increase in U.S. defense spending, suggesting a 66% increase to $1.5 trillion in 2027. While such a sharp increase still needs to go through congress and doesn’t seem likely to pass, military spending is clearly on the rise. Increasing geopolitical tensions are positive for OSS’s demand.

Recent contract wins underline OSS’s positioning. OSS has announced a prototype order worth $1.2 million for U.S. Army combat vehicles already in 2026, following other deals for Safran, a Canadian aerospace company, and companies in the U.S. defense industry in 2025. OSS’s bookings are in good order especially due to orders from the U.S. Army and the U.S. Navy, providing increased visibility as many of the deals are multi-year agreements.

Increasing bookings have already started to show up in OSS’s earnings. The OSS segment, excluding Bressner, generated $9.3 million in revenue in Q3. The figure is up by 43.4% year-on-year, accelerating massively from very moderate growth still in Q2. The gross margin improved by 240 basis points to 45.6% when excluding inventory charges in Q3’2024, reflecting OSS’s newer deals’ better margin profile. OSS’s raised 2025 guidance suggests 24.6% mid-point revenue growth for Q4, still including both the OSS and Bressner businesses. The OSS segment is likely to show good growth in the quarter.

Good bookings provide a very good growth platform for 2026 as well. Multi-year deals in the defense sector, current secular trends in AI/ML edge computing, and increasing military spending make OSS’s growth outlook healthy for the next several years.

OSS has a healthy balance sheet to support growth investments for now. Accounting for $22.4 million divestment proceeds from Bressner and a $12.5 million equity raise in October, the company has approximately $39.2 million in net cash.

Inconsistent Profitability Is a Major Issue

OSS’s profitability is still a major issue. The company’s earnings have been thin and largely negative during the past decade; OSS’s earnings power is very volatile and inconsistent. On top, Bressner has historically had much better profitability than the OSS business, for example generating $2.3 million in 2024 operating income against OSS’s deeply negative income – the most profitable part of the company has now been divested. The OSS segment generated a -$0.4 million operating loss in Q3 despite having an unusually high 45.6% gross margin, slightly above the company’s “mid-30s to low-to-mid-40s” communicated target range.

Operating expenses have grown significantly as OSS has invested heavily in R&D in 2025 to gain new deals, still offsetting a good part of underlying operating leverage in Q3.

Ultimately, the question is where OSS is able to expand its earnings. A good growth outlook does provide OSS with significant room for operating leverage, and I believe that the boost could now make OSS profitable more sustainably. OSS expects some moderation in R&D expenses in 2026, and when coupled with good revenue growth, the margin outlook is very clearly improving. Revenues are finally growing past OSS’s operating cost base.

An inconsistent earnings history and a volatile financial outlook still make the earnings outlook highly uncertain, being a significant risk. OSS’s business relies on constant R&D, and its part in the value chain isn’t cemented – designing frames for third-party computing hardware doesn’t seem to have a very good moat. OSS has to compete with customers’ in-house development, computing vendors, and direct competitors, lowering bargaining power and likely to keep margins modest indefinitely.

OSS’s Valuation Is Unjustified by Financial Outlook

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for OSS. I only factor in the OSS segment in the DCF model.

Industry tailwinds provide OSS a significantly good growth outlook. I estimate the 2024-2034 revenue CAGR at 14.2%, consisting of especially high growth in the next few years but a gradual slowdown in the growth rate. I assume perpetual growth at 3% after the estimate period.

Operating leverage provides a healthier margin outlook for OSS, and I assume the EBIT margin to reach 15% from 2029 forward. The margin estimate is highly volatile, depending on OSS’s operating expense control, gross margin performance, and revenue growth. For comparison, the highest historical EBIT margin that OSS has reported is 2.8% in 2021 - the model assumes a very good margin performance.

I assume a good cash flow conversion after taxes, mainly weakened by OSS’s working capital increases to support growth.

The estimates put OSS’s fair value at $5.0, suggesting massive 56% downside as of writing. OSS’s current valuation is not justified by a likely cash flow scenario, making the investment’s risk-to-reward very weak in my opinion. The current valuation already implies that OSS manages to capture an excessively large number of new, large deals with high gross margins.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.68%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume OSS to leverage a noteworthy amount of debt over the long term. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.20% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha estimates OSS’s beta at 1.67. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.68%.

Takeaway

OSS has captured the market’s interest as macroeconomic tailwinds have aided the company’s revenue outlook. Despite good growth, I believe that OSS is a bad investment at the current price – the company’s earnings history is inconsistent, and the future margin outlook is also highly uncertain. Very ambitious expectations are already priced in at OSS’s current valuation. As such, I initiate OSS at a Sell rating.

data.title: Hoegh Autoliners: Perhaps It Isn't The Right Time Yet
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been ages since I last discussed Hoegh Autoliners (HOEGF) here on Seeking Alpha, it’s perhaps time to have another look at this car transportation vessel operator. I initially covered the company in 2023, and it was a good trade as the share price initially doubled.

Times have changed and additional capacity has hit the water, so this would be a good moment to have another look at the stock.

Hoegh Autoliners has its primary listing in Norway where it is trading on Euronext Oslo with HAUTO as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Norway is just over half a million shares. The current market capitalization is approximately 19B NOK, which translates into approximately $1.9B.

The results

Before discussing the near-term and longer term outlook of the company, I wanted to take a step back and have a look at the Q3 2025 results. During that quarter, the company reported a total revenue of just over $370M, which resulted in an operating profit of approximately $155.1M as the benefit of slightly lower bunker expenses were cancelled out by higher voyage expenses and the higher charter hire expenses (which also helped to post a YoY revenue increase).

The operating profit decrease was generally expected as charter rates have come off and the situation is getting tougher (I’ll discuss this in the next section of this article), but thanks to Hoegh’s very strong balance sheet, the company of course remained profitable as the net interest expense came in at just $10M during the quarter.

The bottom line result showed a net profit of $131M (including the $20M impact related to the gain on the sale of a vessel) for an EPS of $0.69. This also brought the total EPS in the first nine months of the year to $2.14. Hoegh Autoliners is maintaining its dividend policy and the relatively low Q3 dividend reflects the lower visibility on earnings.

Also important: as the cash flows remain strong, and this will of course enable Hoegh to take delivery of the additional Aurora-class vessels in the next few years.

Good results, but I’m cautious

Back in 2023, I expected the company to benefit from a surge in the charter rates, and as you can see below, we are definitely in a downward cycle. Hoegh Autoliners definitely benefited from the surge (and that was the main thesis of my original article), but investors have to be mindful that future contract negotiations will likely happen at lower daily charter rates. And as more supply will be added to the world fleet, I think the charter rates will remain under pressure in the foreseeable future.

Investors have been spoiled, thanks to Hoegh’s smart decision to lock in charter rates for longer periods. This should allow for a soft landing (not in the least because Hoegh signed several new contracts in 2024 at rates that should be higher than the current 1 year charter rates), but Hoegh has to deal with several other items as well.

One important item would be the additional US port fees, which have an estimated yearly impact of $60-70M on Hoegh. The Q4 2025 performance was estimated to be slightly lower than the Q3 EBITDA level, but the $20M USTR impact will have an additional negative influence on the results.

Investment thesis

So while the financial results of Hoegh Autoliners remain robust, it’s important to avoid investing with a rearview mirror. I remain on the sidelines of Hoegh, and I initially thought a ‘hold’ rating was the best approach here.

That being said, given the robust contracting at higher rates, Hoegh could be seen as a valid candidate to ‘ride out the storm’. The company enjoys an excellent reputation (which is helpful when you’re negotiating contracts with car manufacturers) and as the world economy improves, car sale volumes may increase, which would put Hoegh Autoliners in a prime position to benefit.

There’s a reason why the stock is trading at just 4-4.5 times its annualized Q3 earnings (adjusted for non-recurring items like the sale of a vessel), and the stock is a ‘speculative buy’ at best right now. It’s a good call option on improving charter conditions, but given the anticipated vessel supply increase for the next two years, investors will likely have to be patient.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chubb: A Steady Compounder And Long Term Buy
data.description:
Why I Invest in Insurance Companies in General

Property and casualty insurance is among the oldest modern industries and remains one of the few large industries with structurally favorable economics, where quiet compounding takes place for patient value investors.

The reason is simple. At its core, insurance is, by design, a “reverse lottery.” Premiums are collected first, much like the money people part with when they buy lottery tickets. Claims are paid later, to a small portion of policyholders who experience losses, not to encourage gambling but to share misfortune.

The gap between premiums collected and claims paid out — the “float” — becomes a continuously replenished pool of capital that the insurer can deploy to generate investment returns. This is essentially how Warren Buffett leveraged the float from GEICO to build Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B).

The real challenge for us average investors is to find a competent operator of the insurance business and patiently stick with them for decades.

How Chubb Caught My Attention In Particular

Talking about Buffett, it was Berkshire Hathaway’s $6 billion plus purchase of about 6% of the shares of Chubb (CB) stock that piqued my interest and made me acquire the shares of Chubb at around $270 per share.

I readily admit that my view here may have been influenced by my faith in the Oracle of Omaha. I am also acutely aware that Mr. Buffett is not infallible, as I discussed in this article on Kraft Heinz. Still, as the saying goes, “it takes a fox to know a fox,” and I find it hard to believe that Mr. Buffett — a consummate operator of insurance businesses — would place a $6 billion bet on Chubb unless he believed it to be a worthy competitor.

However, we still need to do our own due diligence to understand Chubb, a global property and casualty insurer with a market cap around $120 billion today. Its business covers commercial insurance, high-net-worth personal lines, specialty insurance, and reinsurance. It is a large organization with complex lines of businesses, which has made the company and its stock more difficult for individual investors to analyze than, for example, a single-asset biotech company.

In this piece, I will try to break down this daunting task into a few segments: Chubb’s stock valuation, its revenue growth trajectory, and its profitability profile, to essentially address one question whose answer has eluded me for a long time: how has such a large insurer sustained such a long track record of underwriting outperformance over its peers when insurance is essentially a commodity service?

As usual, I will do it by comparing the stock in question to its peers of comparable size.

Valuation of the Peers

Overall, as compared against Travelers (TRV), Allstate (ALL), Progressive (PGR), and Hartford (HIG), Chubb appears to be reasonably valued relative to its book value, sales and profits. By no means it is richly valued, whether compared against its peers or the overall stock market today. Chubb’s price-to-book is the lowest among the peers at 1.68, less than half of Progressive’s 3.68. Progressive’s stock price, as analyzed in this piece, is probably still riding the tail winds of its revenue and profit growths accumulated in the last hard market and may have more room to fall as the market is turning softer.

In terms of EV or market cap to sales, the variance among the peers becomes more pronounced. For example, Chubb commands a P/S of 2.09, followed by Progressive’s 1.48, Hartford’s 1.39, and Travelers’ 1.32. We will see below whether Chubb’s higher ratios here are justified by its higher profitability.

Growth Trajectory of the Peers

Let's now review the growth trajectories of the peers from Seeking Alpha's peer comparison function.

In terms of revenue growth, Chubb has grown its revenue at 11% and 12.5% CAGR during the last five and three years, respectively, slower than Progressive’s 15.2% and 20.6%. However, it has grown faster and more steadily than the rest of the pack. It also appears that the insurance market is heading into a soft market, as YoY and forward revenue growth for all the peers except Progressive are now reduced to mid-single digits. This is something an astute investor of insurers needs to pay attention to.

In terms of profit growth, whether measured by EPS, net income, EBIT, or EBITDA, Chubb has been growing during the last three years or so at around 20%. At first glance, you may conclude that Chubb has been growing its profits much slower than Progressive and Allstate. A closer look at the earnings of Progressive (as explained in my piece in Progressive) and Allstate reveals that it was because they had rather low points to start with. That is, Chubb’s earnings growth has been steadier over the years.

Here, Chubb’s profit has shrunk by 2% YoY, and therefore it appears that the analyst community has projected much slower profit growth rates for Chubb at 10% than all its peers here: 15% for Hartford, 36% for Progressive, and over 100% for Allstate, apparently expecting Allstate to turn around after struggling with its profitability lately.

In terms of book value, Chubb has been growing at a respectable 20% CAGR over the last three years, behind Progressive’s 36% and Allstate’s 22%, and slightly ahead of the other peers.

In terms of total assets, Chubb has been growing at a respectable 11% over the last three years, behind Progressive’s 17% and moderately ahead of the other peers.

A fair assessment here is that Chubb appears not to grow as aggressively during the current hard market cycle (which is probably turning into a soft market now). However, Chubb’s growth is steadier, whether measured from the top line or the bottom line, or by income statement or balance sheet.

Profitability of the Peers

A better metric of profitability for the insurance industry in general is the so-called combined ratio, which is the sum of the loss ratio (the total amount of insurance claim payouts divided by the total amount of premium revenue) and the expense ratio (the total amount of expenses managing the business — such as advertising, commissions, salaries, rent, and legal fees — divided by the total amount of premium revenue).

Here I depart from Seeking Alpha’s peer comparison function and use combined ratio to measure Chubb’s profitability first over time and against its peers.

First, let’s turn to Chubb’s December 2025 corporate presentation. At Slide 6, the company proudly wrote about its P&C segment:

World-class combined ratio advantage vs peers across market cycle–

- 10 years: 8% points

- 5 years: 7% points

– 3 years: 7% points

– 1 year: 6% points

admin ratio leadership vs peers – 8.2% vs 14.2% peers over last five years.

The footnote states the peers for this combined ratio comparison are AIG, Berkshire Hathaway, CNA, Hartford, Liberty Mutual, and Travelers, and the peers for admin ratio comparison are AIG, Berkshire Hathaway, CNA, Hartford, and Travelers, all publicly traded large and reputable insurers.

The above shows how much better Chubb has been doing in terms of profitability over its peers. I still went on to dig out Chubb’s actual combined ratios from its 2019 to 2022 annual reports, news reports, and its 3Q/2025 quarterly earnings release, and compared them against those of Progressive.

Year Progressive Combined Ratio Chubb Combined Ratio Delta

2023 94.90% 86.50% -8.40%
2022 95.80% 87.60% -8.20%
2021 95.30% 89.10% -6.20%
2020 87.70% 96.10% 8.40%
2019 90.90% 90.60% -0.30%
2018 90.60% 90.60% 0.00%
2017 93.40% 94.70% 1.30%
Average 91.88% 89.29%

From here we can see that although on average Chubb’s combined ratio is only about 2% lower than Progressive’s, during the last five years or so Chubb's lead has widened and become consistent. It is also worthy of noting that the last five years have been a hard market favoring insurers.

The combined ratios of Chubb, whether viewed longitudinally against its historical track record, or against its peers, strongly suggest that Chubb has struck a very deliberate and healthy balance between the competing goals of growing its revenue while maintaining its profitability, whereas competitors, including Progressive, may have grown their revenues more aggressively during the hard market cycle at the expense of profitability.

Going back to Mr. Buffett’s investment in Chubb, the title of this Wall Street Journal article probably told us all: What Does Buffett See in Chubb? Maybe Just Good Underwriting.

Among other things, the article also noted in particular that

Chubb’s combined ratio in 2023 at 86.5% was far better than the nearly 102% combined ratio Fitch Ratings calculated across the U.S. property-and-casualty industry that year...

Chubb is also comfortably below the U.S. commercial lines average, which was 96% in 2023, according to Fitch.

So, we now have confirmation that the higher price-to-sales ratio of Chubb is well supported by its high and consistent profit margin from its underwriting business.

Probably more importantly, we have confirmed that while the stock of Chubb is reasonably priced as measured against its earnings, sales, and book values, Chubb has also grown its business during the last five years at decent and steady rates, whether measured by revenue, book value, or total assets.

Why Chubb’s underwriting performance is not easily replicable

Given its sheer scale of operations, one has to wonder how Chubb has been able to consistently grow its already-strong underwriting results. Chubb’s operating record is consistent with an insurer that has embedded underwriting discipline into its institutional structure. After watching various interviews, including this one with its CEO, Evan G. Greenberg, and reading its SEC filings, I believe the following are responsible for Chubb’s excellent underwriting practices and operating results.

First, Chubb developed and preserved an underwriting-first identity. Over decades, leadership succession has largely favored executives formed inside the underwriting function. When underwriting quality defines advancement, short-term premium/revenue growth ceases to be the primary currency. This aligns management attention with underwriting quality rather than premium volume.

Second, compensation structures appear to have lagged risk rather than front-run it. Chubb’s sustained performance in long-tail lines implies compensation and evaluation systems that exposed decision-makers to multi-year outcomes — through deferred incentives and career advancement (see the paragraph above) tied to such outcomes.

Third, underwriting authority remained centralized even as operations expanded. Most large insurers eventually devolve into loosely connected product groups and regional profit centers. As that happens, risk standards and pricing discipline erode under short term revenue pressure. Chubb apparently has avoided that fate so far.

Fourth, Chubb's admin ratio at 8.2% vs 14.2% of its peers certainly has contributed to its bottom line directly. It indicates that despite its size and global footprint, Chubb has been well run as a cost-effective tight ship.

None of these will erode quickly, or be easily replicated by competitors, especially large competitors whose institutional cultures have evolved differently over the decades.

By the way, if this sounds familiar, you may have read my article on Kinsale. Either Kinsale has borrowed Chubb’s playbook in setting up proper employee incentives, retaining underwriting autonomy, enforcing underwriting discipline, and controlling administrative costs, or — as the saying goes — “all happy families are the same.” Perhaps all successful insurance companies, large and small, are the same.

Why Chubb fits Berkshire unusually well

Now I will share my view as to why Berkshire Hathaway has invested in the shares of Chubb.

First, Berkshire Hathaway currently sits atop over $300 billion of cash and cash equivalents. It has become increasingly difficult to find investments large enough to meaningfully move the needle for a trillion-dollar enterprise, compared with the earlier days when mispriced mid-cap opportunities could drive exponential growth. Large-scale operating businesses that can absorb substantial capital while maintaining stable profitability have therefore become increasingly relevant within Berkshire’s opportunity set, and Chubb appears to be a viable candidate.

Second, Chubb is a successful insurance franchise, global in scope, with an underwriting business that produces the kind of stable and steadily-growing results Berkshire has historically sought in its portfolio of operating businesses.

Third, Berkshire’s insurance operations have for decades occupied a central role in its business model, particularly because of the economic value of insurance “float,” as exemplified by GEICO, in supporting Berkshire’s investment activities that generate additional returns for its shareholders. Expanding the scale of high-quality insurance operations, whether through a passive stake in Chubb or otherwise, would be consistent with Berkshire’s long-standing business model of using "float" to generate additional returns on its capital.

Slightly larger than GEICO as measured by annual revenue, if Chubb were to be acquired by Berkshire, which is a hypothetical thought on my part not supported by any evidence, it would meaningfully expand Berkshire’s position in the global insurance market and, more importantly, increase the size of its fuel tank — the float that will generate long-term returns for the capital of the broader enterprise.

Of course, the fact that Berkshire Hathaway has acquired a $6 billion plus stake in Chubb, or the possibility that it may increase its stake, should not by itself be a reason for an investor to buy the shares of Chubb. The more important reason remains the one that likely drew Berkshire to the company in the first place: the long-term value of a large-scale, industry-leading insurance underwriting business.

Risks of Investing in Chubb

In the short term, as the insurance market moves into a softer cycle (see the chart below), it is possible that the revenue and earnings growth of all insurers, including Chubb, will come under pressure, which could in turn adversely affect its stock price.

In the intermediate to long term, there may be some key-man risk, as Mr. Evan G. Greenberg has been Chubb’s forceful and charismatic leader since long before the ACE and Chubb merger in 2015.

In the long term, Chubb, like any large organization, may gradually succumb to short-term economic pressures and bureaucratic tendencies, thereby losing its underwriting discipline, its core competitive edge.

What Investors May Do with Chubb, Kinsale, and Progressive

One thing that stands out when you look at Chubb’s history is not only how fast it has grown, but how evenly it has grown. Its premium growth has usually been more steady than aggressive.

This matters as the property and casualty market gradually turns softer after several years of firm pricing. In softer markets, insurers that depend more heavily on volume tend to experience much wider swings in underwriting results. Chubb’s operating record points to a greater willingness to restrain growth to preserve margin. That should translate into lower volatility in revenue and profitability and, consequently, stock price relative to peers.

For the investors whose primary objective is no longer maximum upside but durable capital preservation with steady real growth, Chubb may be an ideal compounder over long periods. It is the tortoise, not the hare, and it is very unlikely to give you any large negative surprise.

There is also a portfolio design angle here. Today’s stock market is highly valued and concentrated in a handful of technology companies, which, viewed with a long-term lens, has made a correction and/or long-term stagnation more and more likely. For investors who are retired or near retirement, that makes Chubb, a textbook value stock with reasonable valuation and steady growth, a decent hedge or even an anchor against an overvalued market.

For younger investors with longer time horizons and higher risk tolerance, faster-growing specialty insurers such as Kinsale may warrant heavier weightings. After all, Chubb is not a small-cap opportunity and is unlikely to deliver explosive growth. However, Chubb still deserves a place there, and dollar-averaging investing may be deployed to manage the risks of acquiring Chubb shares at cyclical high points.

For those who follow both the insurance market cycle and the stock market closely, they may consider waiting for a softer market environment to capture better entry points in well-managed but somewhat more volatile insurers such as Progressive.

data.title: Duolingo's Rule Of 69.5% Outperformance Meets Growth Headwinds And Overstretched Valuations
data.description:
In this article, I shall discuss why I am initiating a Hold rating on Duolingo (DUOL), with its profitable growth trends across the Rule of 69.5% outperformance negated by the expensive valuations, the deteriorating gross margins from the intensified AI initiatives, and the decelerating growth profile.

This is worsened by the potential volatility arising from the elevated short interest ratio and the ongoing shareholder equity erosion from the elevated SBC expenses—warranting caution for interested investors.

DUOL Faces Growth Headwinds

For the uninitiated, Duolingo is a virtual learning education platform that is touted to be “the world’s most popular way to learn languages and the top-grossing Education app in the app stores,” with an estimated online/offline market size of $123B in 2027.

Given that DUOL offers courses in numerous languages, demand for its online learning courses appears to be robust, as observed in the last reported performance metrics in FQ3'25, including:

  • the growing total bookings of $281.9M (+33.2% YoY),
  • the Daily Active Users (DAU) growth to 50.5M (+35.7% YoY), and
  • the higher subscription bookings per paid subscriber at an estimated sum of $20.89 (+1.9% YoY), based on the Subscription bookings of $240.3M (+36.3% YoY) and the Paid Subscribers of 11.5M (+33.7% YoY).

Even then, it is undeniable that DUOL's growth profile has seemingly decelerated, as observed in the tougher comparison from FQ3'24 booking growth at +37.6% YoY and DAU growth at +53.7% YoY.

While the company may still report an expanding Paid Subscriber Penetration at 9% in FQ3'25 (+0.5 points YoY), it appears that the platform may be facing the law of large numbers, where “a large entity that is growing rapidly can't maintain that growth pace forever.”

This is worsened by the unexpected backlash from the DUOL CEO's April 2025 letter, with the focus to “become an AI-first organization, including phasing out contractors if AI could do their work, and giving a team the ability to hire a new person only if they were not able to automate their work through AI,” resulting in the lower than expected FQ2'25 DAU growth:

We came in at 40%, which is slightly on the lower end of what we thought. Everything is -- we feel very strong about this. The reason we came towards the lower end was because I said some stuff about AI, and this was -- I didn't give enough context. And because of that, we got, particularly in our social media, we got some backlash on it. (Seeking Alpha)

Combined with the decelerating booking growth of +33.2% YoY in FQ3'25, +40.9% YoY in FQ2'25, and +37.5% YoY in FQ1'25, against the +40% YoY reported in FY2024 and the historical growth at a 3Y CAGR of +44%, I can understand why DUOL has suffered the deep sell-off since the 52-week highs.

This is worsened by the impact of the strategic shift in longer-term initiatives, the competition risks from copycat apps, and the management hinting at DAU deceleration growth at +30% YoY in FQ4'25.

At the same time, while DUOL has been able to introduce “148 new language courses, more than doubling its previous offerings,” thanks to the use of generative AI, this initiative has also triggered the moderation in its YTD gross margins to 72% (-1.1 points YoY), attributed to “an increase in generative AI costs associated with expansion of our Duolingo Max subscription tier and increased hosting costs.”

Therefore, while the platform has reported decent advertising monetization opportunities and benefited from the gamification approach along with the ongoing diversification of its payment service providers from Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) to Stripe at 10.8% in FQ3'25 to save on provider fees, I am of the opinion that FY2026 is likely to bring a tougher year for DUOL, which perhaps explains the stock's recent underperformance.

DUOL's Profitable Growth Trends Negated By Premium Valuations

Thanks to the YTD adj. EPS performance of $2.56 (+63% YoY), after discounting the outsized one-time income-tax benefit in FQ3'25, along with the FY2024 performance at $1.88 (+437.1% YoY), it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat promising.

This is with the consensus projecting a robust bottom-line growth at a 2Y CAGR of +37.2% between the LTM GAAP EPS of $2.84 (+55.1% sequentially) and the consensus FY2027 GAAP EPS estimates of $5.35.

On the other hand, there is no denying that DUOL remains inherently expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 6.93x and my estimated P/E valuations of 62.20x (based on the current stock prices of $176.66 and the LTM EPS of $2.84), with the current FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 20.87x distorted by the one-time income-tax benefit in FQ3'25.

This is compared to the sector median of 1.42x/18.38x, despite the moderation from the 5Y mean of 11.45x/287.77x, respectively.

Part of DUOL's premium valuations may be attributed to its Rule of 69.5% outperformance in FQ3'25, compared to a year ago at Rule of 66.5%, based on the FQ3'25 revenue growth by +41% YoY (against a year ago at +39.9% YoY) and the Free Cash Flow margin of 28.5% (+1.9 points YoY).

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing DUOL's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 6.93x and the Rule of 69.5% outperformance to its education/virtual learning platform competitors, including:

  • Pearson (PSO) at 2.20x/11% (based on H1'25 revenue growth at +2% YoY and H1'25 FCF margins of 9%),
  • Chegg, Inc. (CHGG) at 0.24x/-44.1% (FQ3'25 at -43% YoY/-1.1%),
  • Coursera (COUR) at 0.57x/23.6% (FQ3'25 at +10% YoY/+13.6%),
  • Udemy (UDMY) at 0.60x/6.1% (FQ3'25 at 0% YoY/6.1%), and
  • Nerdy, Inc. (NRDY) at 0.61x/-16.3% (FQ3'25 at -1%/-15.3%), respectively,

with it apparent that DUOL's high-growth investment thesis has been well buoyed, as the bulls defend a trading floor at $166s during the worst of the November 2025 correction.

This may be partly aided by the company's healthy balance sheet with a net cash position of $1.11B (+26.1% YoY) and zero debts, aside from operating leases.

DUOL Is A Hold Here

For now, after hitting a new peak of $544s by May 2025, DUOL has already breached numerous support levels and the 50/100/200-day moving averages by the time of writing, albeit seemingly finding a trading floor at the $170s since early November 2025.

Even so, despite losing much of its value from the prior meltdown and the notably oversold technical indicators across the low RSIs/low trading volumes, I believe that it may be more prudent to initiate a Hold (Neutral) rating for the DUOL stock here.

My caution is attributed to DUOL's rather expensive estimated 2Y PEG ratio of 1.67x, based on my estimated P/E valuations of 62.20x and the projected EPS expansion at a 2Y CAGR of +37.2% as discussed above, against the sector median of 0.97x and its direct peers, including:

  • PSO at 1.51x (using the same methodology),
  • CHGG at 0x (from LTM levels of $0.21 to FY2027 estimates of $0.21),
  • COUR at 0.99x, and
  • UDMY at 2.33x,

with it apparent that there is a minimal margin of safety despite the -67.5% meltdown from the 52-week highs, barring an acceleration in DUOL's EPS growth in the high double digits, worsened by the potential near-term volatility arising from the elevated short interest ratio at 12.42% and the beta coefficient of 1.63x.

The bullish support at current levels of $170s implies that DUOL is likely to trade sideways as it grows into its premium valuations as well, with its future return prospects likely to remain mixed in the near term.

While I am impressed with DUOL's successful monetization across existing/new subscribers and the expanding ARPUs, readers must also note the risks of shareholder equity erosion arising from the elevated SBC expenses at 13.7% of revenues (-1.8 points YoY) and the growing shares outstanding by +2.4% YoY (building upon a year ago at +2.1% YoY).

It may be more prudent to stay on the sidelines for now.
